senses.definition: People who behave in a showy or affected way to create a certain impression.
examples: The gallery was full of attitudinarians striking dramatic poses for the cameras.
Critics dismissed them as mere attitudinarians with no real commitment to change.

senses.definition: To behave in a way that is overly posed or affected in order to impress people.
examples: He prefers to attitudinise on social media rather than do any real work.
The actor tended to attitudinise instead of showing true emotion on stage.

senses.definition: Behaved in an overly posed or affected way in order to impress people.
examples: She attitudinised throughout the interview, clearly more interested in image than ideas.
They attitudinised on stage instead of engaging with the audience.

senses.definition: Showing a self-conscious, artificial style or attitude.
examples: The performance felt stiff and attitudinised.
His writing has an oddly attitudinised tone that distracts from the message.

senses.definition: A person who often behaves in an artificial or showy way to impress others.
examples: The critic called him an attitudiniser rather than a real artist.
Only an attitudiniser would dress like that for a serious debate.

senses.definition: People who often behave in an artificial or showy way to impress others.
examples: The movement’s leaders were dismissed as mere attitudinisers.
Online debates sometimes attract attitudinisers rather than serious thinkers.

senses.definition: Behaves in an overly posed or affected way to impress people.
examples: He constantly attitudinises on social media.
She attitudinises whenever a camera is near.

senses.definition: Behaving in a self‑conscious, posed, or affected way to create an impression.
examples: Instead of helping, he was busy attitudinising for his followers.
She spent the evening attitudinising at the bar.

senses.definition: The act of behaving in an artificial or showy way to impress others.
examples: All that political attitudinising hides a lack of real policy.
The play was spoiled by the actor’s constant attitudinising.

senses.definition: Repeated acts of behaving in an artificial or showy way to impress others.
examples: His various attitudinisings soon bored the audience.
The book mocks the attitudinisings of social elites.

senses.definition: To adopt a showy or unnatural pose or opinion, especially to impress others.
examples: Instead of speaking plainly, he began to attitudinize about morality.
The performer tended to attitudinize rather than connect with the audience.

senses.definition: Adopted a showy or unnatural pose or opinion, especially to impress others.
examples: He attitudinized throughout the debate instead of answering questions.
They attitudinized when the cameras arrived.

senses.definition: Having a self‑consciously artificial or affected style or manner.
examples: The film’s dialogue feels stiff and attitudinized.
Her prose has an oddly attitudinized quality.

senses.definition: A person who often adopts showy or artificial attitudes to impress others.
examples: He is more of an attitudinizer than a serious thinker.
The article describes social‑media influencers as professional attitudinizers.

senses.definition: People who frequently adopt showy or artificial attitudes to impress others.
examples: The conference drew a crowd of attitudinizers and self‑promoters.
Some commentators dismiss activists as mere attitudinizers.

senses.definition: Adopts showy or artificial attitudes, especially to impress others.
examples: He attitudinizes whenever journalists are present.
She attitudinizes about fairness but never helps anyone.

senses.definition: Adopting showy or artificial attitudes, especially in a repeated or obvious way.
examples: They spent the evening attitudinizing instead of listening.
He kept attitudinizing in front of the mirror.

senses.definition: The act or habit of behaving in a showy, artificial way to impress others.
examples: The article criticises the empty attitudinizing of public figures.
There was more attitudinizing than honest discussion in the meeting.

senses.definition: Repeated or varied acts of behaving in a showy, artificial way.
examples: His various attitudinizings soon became tiresome.
The book mocks the attitudinizings of celebrity activists.

senses.definition: A person who is mainly concerned with having or showing a particular attitude or style.
examples: He is an attitudist who values image over substance.
The film follows a group of young attitudists in the city.

senses.definition: An alternate or variant name used historically for the Neutral or Neutral Huron people, an Iroquoian‑speaking Indigenous group of North America.
examples: Some early French sources use the term Attiwendaronk for the Neutral Nation.
Linguists study the language once spoken by the Attiwendaronk.

senses.definition: Waste rock or worthless material left over after mining or quarrying.
examples: The valley was filled with old heaps of attle from the mine.
They used the attle as cheap fill for the road.

senses.definition: Used at the top of a letter or email to show who it is mainly for (short for "attention").
examples: Please send the documents to: Attn: Human Resources Department.
The envelope was marked "Attn: Sales Manager".

senses.definition: A rare or specialised abbreviation whose meaning is not established in general contemporary English.
examples: The string "attntrp" appears in the dataset but is not used in normal writing.
Learners are unlikely to meet the form "attntrp" in everyday English.

senses.definition: A very powerful laser that produces extremely short light pulses measured in attoseconds.
examples: The lab uses an attolaser to study ultrafast electron motion.
Building an attolaser requires very advanced optics.

senses.definition: Very powerful lasers that produce extremely short light pulses measured in attoseconds.
examples: Attolasers are opening new fields of research in ultrafast physics.
Only a few laboratories in the world operate attolasers.

senses.definition: A Latin‑based term meaning "lifting" or "raising", mainly used within set Latin phrases in technical or scholarly writing.
examples: The phrase "attollens humeros" appears in the Latin commentary.
Attollens is rarely used outside specialised contexts.

senses.definition: A Latin‑based form meaning "raising" or "lifting", found only in highly specialised or historical contexts.
examples: The manuscript preserves the archaic form attollent.
Students of medieval Latin may encounter terms like attollent.

senses.definition: Very rare plural form of a Latin‑based word, only seen in technical or historical discussion of Latin forms.
examples: The glossary lists several attollents and related forms.
Most learners will never need to use attollents.

senses.definition: A very tiny person or thing; an archaic or dialect spelling of "atomy".
examples: The child was an attomy beside the huge door.
He felt like an attomy in the vast empty hall.

senses.definition: An old or dialect spelling of "at once", meaning immediately or at the same time.
examples: He answered attonce and without fear.
All the bells began to ring attonce.

senses.definition: An archaic spelling of "atone", meaning to do something to show you are sorry for a wrong.
examples: He hoped to attone for his past mistakes.
She gave money to charity to attone for her lies.

senses.definition: Archaic past tense of "attone" or "atone", meaning showed you were sorry by doing something to make up for a wrong.
examples: He attoned for his crimes by years of service.
She attoned with a sincere apology.

senses.definition: Archaic third person form of "attone" or "atone", meaning does something to make up for a wrong.
examples: He attones for his pride by helping the poor.
She attones with quiet acts of kindness.

senses.definition: Archaic form of "atoning", meaning doing something to show you are sorry and to make up for a wrong.
examples: He spent his life attoning for a single mistake.
She felt she could never stop attoning for what she had done.

senses.definition: A branch of physics that studies extremely fast processes using attosecond laser pulses.
examples: Attophysics allows scientists to watch electrons move inside atoms.
She is doing her PhD in attophysics at a leading research institute.

senses.definition: In law, to formally agree to become the tenant of a new landlord or to transfer a legal duty or loyalty to another person.
examples: The tenants agreed to attorn to the new owner of the building.
The clause required the borrower to attorn to any future lender.

senses.definition: An old legal form related to "attorn", used historically in legal Latin or Law French to mean turn over or transfer.
examples: The medieval document uses the verb attornare in a lease formula.
Scholars debate how often attornare appeared in practice.

senses.definition: In law, agreed to become the tenant of a new landlord or transferred a legal duty or loyalty to another person.
examples: The tenants attorned to the bank after the foreclosure.
He attorned to the new landlord without objection.

senses.definition: A person who is allowed by law to act for someone else in legal matters, especially a lawyer in the US.
examples: She hired an attorney to represent her in court.
You should talk to an attorney before signing the contract.

senses.definition: All attorneys as a group, or the profession or world of attorneys, often used humorously or critically.
examples: The reform was widely discussed in attorneydom.
He wrote a satirical book about the culture of attorneydom.

senses.definition: Different groups or worlds of attorneys, often used humorously or critically.
examples: The article compared corporate and criminal attorneydoms.
Various national attorneydoms reacted differently to the ruling.

senses.definition: Non‑standard or playful use meaning acted as an attorney for someone.
examples: She attorneyed for several small charities early in her career.
He attorneyed the case alone from start to finish.

senses.definition: Informal term for working or practising as an attorney.
examples: After years of hard attorneying, she switched to teaching.
He spends most of his time attorneying in the city courts.

senses.definition: A typical habit, manner, or way of speaking associated with attorneys, often seen as negative.
examples: His speech was full of legal jargon and other attorneyisms.
Critics complained about the attorneyism in her careful answers.

senses.definition: Typical habits, manners, or ways of speaking associated with attorneys, often seen as negative.
examples: The book mocks the attorneyisms of corporate lawyers.
His answers were full of cautious attorneyisms.

senses.definition: People who are allowed by law to act for others in legal matters, especially lawyers in the US.
examples: The company’s attorneys reviewed the agreement.
Many attorneys now specialise in environmental law.

senses.definition: The job, position, or state of being an attorney.
examples: He was proud of his long attorneyship at the firm.
Her attorneyship focused on public‑interest cases.

senses.definition: Jobs, positions, or periods of working as an attorney.
examples: Their attorneyships took them to several different cities.
The book compares three very different attorneyships.

senses.definition: Formally agreeing in law to recognise a new landlord or to transfer a legal duty or loyalty to another person.
examples: The tenants are attorning to the new property owner under the clause.
By attorning, they agreed to pay rent directly to the bank.

senses.definition: A legal act in which a tenant formally agrees to recognise a new landlord or owner as the person to whom rent is paid.
examples: The lease included a subordination and attornment clause.
Attornment can protect the new owner’s right to receive rent.

senses.definition: Legal acts in which tenants formally agree to recognise new landlords or owners.
examples: The statute regulates attornments after a property sale.
Several attornments were recorded when the building changed hands.

senses.definition: Formally agrees in law to recognise a new landlord or to transfer a legal duty or loyalty to another person.
examples: Under this clause, the tenant attorns to any new owner.
If the borrower defaults, the tenant attorns to the lender.

senses.definition: Obsolete or specialised term from French meaning a touch or contact, sometimes used in legal or medical contexts.
examples: The old report refers to an attouchement between the parties.
In some texts, attouchement is used to describe physical contact.
